verses.text: God sends Jeremiah to the king's house: do justice, deliver the oppressed; obey, and the throne shall stand; refuse, and this house shall be desolate. Obedience secures the throne. God's promises to the king hang on his doing right.

Though the palace is as Gilead and Lebanon to Him, He will make it a wilderness, and nations will ask why; the answer is, 'they forsook the covenant.' Beauty is no protection. The cause of every ruin is the forsaking of God.

Weep not for the dead king but for Shallum, carried away never to return. Exile is worse than death. To be torn from God's land and never see it again is a bitter doom.
Weep not for the dead king but for Shallum, carried away never to return. Exile is worse than death. To be torn from God's land and never see it again is a bitter doom.

Woe to Jehoiakim, who builds by unrighteousness and forced labour; his father did justice and it was well with him, but 'thine eyes... are... for covetousness.' Greed condemns him. To know God is to plead the cause of the poor, not to exploit them.

Jehoiakim shall have no mourning, but 'the burial of an ass, drawn and cast forth.' His end is dishonour. The king who oppressed his people dies unlamented.
Jehoiakim shall have no mourning, but 'the burial of an ass, drawn and cast forth.' His end is dishonour. The king who oppressed his people dies unlamented.
Jerusalem's lovers are destroyed; she who dwelt at ease in cedar palaces will groan when pangs come. Her comfort fails. Those who nest in luxury find no shelter when judgment strikes.

Though Coniah were 'the signet upon my right hand,' God would pluck him off and hurl him into Babylon. The king is cast away. No rank can hold a man in God's favour once he rebels.

Coniah, a despised broken vessel, is to be written 'childless,' none of his seed prospering on David's throne. The line seems cut off. Yet through Joseph's legal, not natural, descent the throne passed to Christ, keeping the promise.
